**Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research** — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012  
This meta-analysis finds that people perform better and persist more when their vocational interests match the content of their work. It supports treating “interesting work” as a major career variable, but not as the only one.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit** — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005  
This meta-analysis finds that person–job fit is strongly related to job satisfaction, organizational commitment, intention to stay, and performance. It implies that fit between daily tasks and your interests/abilities is likely more important than a small compensation difference.

**Meaningful Work and Well-Being: A Meta-Analysis** — Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling & Tay, 2019  
This meta-analysis finds that meaningful work is robustly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions. It suggests that “interesting” matters most when it also feels meaningful and identity-congruent.

**Motivating Creativity in Organizations: On Doing What You Love and Loving What You Do** — Amabile, 1997  
Amabile argues that intrinsic motivation—doing work because it is interesting, enjoyable, or challenging—is a central input to creativity. It is especially relevant to research roles, where novelty and quality often depend on sustained curiosity rather than external incentives alone.

**Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory** — Hackman & Oldham, 1976  
This classic field study shows that skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback predict internal motivation and satisfaction. It frames “interesting work” as one part of a broader job-design bundle, not a single decision criterion.

**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature** — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007  
This meta-analysis finds that autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and task complexity have large relationships with satisfaction and motivation. It suggests comparing the actual work design of the two roles, not just labels like “research” or “team lead.”

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation** — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999  
This meta-analysis finds that expected tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, especially when experienced as controlling. It cautions against optimizing mainly for salary when the alternative is substantially less interesting work.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature** — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010  
This meta-analysis finds that pay level has only a modest association with job satisfaction, while pay satisfaction matters somewhat more. It suggests that a moderately higher salary may not compensate for a large drop in intrinsic interest or fit.

**Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved** — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023  
This paper reconciles prior findings by showing that experienced well-being generally rises with income on a log scale, but the pattern differs across happier and less-happy people. It means salary is not irrelevant, but marginal income gains at already-high compensation levels should be weighed against daily work experience.

**Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion** — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone, 2006  
This paper argues that people tend to overestimate how much higher income will improve day-to-day happiness because income becomes salient when they think about it. It is direct evidence that compensation can be overweighted in career decisions relative to how work actually feels.

**Self-Concordance, Goal Attainment, and the Pursuit of Happiness: Can There Be an Upward Spiral?** — Sheldon & Houser-Marko, 2001  
This paper finds that goals aligned with a person’s values and interests produce greater sustained effort, goal attainment, and later well-being. It supports choosing the path that best matches what you genuinely want to become, whether that is deeper research or leading people.

**Toward a Theory of Individual Differences and Leadership: Understanding the Motivation to Lead** — Chan & Drasgow, 2001  
This paper develops the concept of motivation to lead and shows that individual differences in wanting leadership roles predict leadership emergence and related outcomes. It suggests that “leading a small team” should count as intrinsic interest if you truly value it, not merely as extrinsic status.

**Challenge and Hindrance Stressors and Job Outcomes: A Meta-Analysis** — LePine, Podsakoff & LePine, 2005  
This meta-analysis distinguishes challenge stressors, which can relate positively to motivation and performance, from hindrance stressors, which are mostly harmful. It suggests team leadership may be energizing if it feels like growth, but costly if it mostly adds bureaucracy or reduced technical depth.

- **Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research** — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012): This meta-analysis finds that alignment between a person’s interests and their work predicts job performance, training performance, and persistence. - **Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit** — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005): Person–job fit and person–organization fit are strongly associated with job satisfaction, commitment, and lower intent to quit. “Interesting work” is one kind of fit, but values, culture, team, manager, and role expectations also matter.

- **The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature** — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010): Pay level has only a relatively weak relationship with overall job satisfaction, while satisfaction with pay is more strongly related. - **Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion** — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone (2006): The paper argues that people often overestimate how much income differences affect experienced happiness. answer:
**Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014)**  
Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts performance, especially quality and complex-task performance. Extrinsic incentives also predict performance, so the evidence supports prioritizing interesting work heavily but not almost exclusively.

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation — Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999)**  
Expected tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks. This suggests salary/status can sometimes crowd out interest, but it does not imply compensation or role structure are irrelevant.

**Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being — Ryan & Deci (2000)**  
Well-being and durable motivation depend on autonomy, competence, and relatedness. A leadership role could score highly on these even if the technical work is less intrinsically fascinating.

**Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory — Hackman & Oldham (1976)**  
Jobs are more motivating when they offer skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. “Interesting work” matters, but it is only one part of a broader job-design picture.

**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007)**  
Autonomy, task significance, feedback, social support, and interdependence are all linked to satisfaction and performance. The paper supports evaluating the full day-to-day role, not just the intellectual topic.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005)**  
Fit with the job, organization, group, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and turnover intentions. This argues against optimizing only for interesting tasks if team, manager, values, or growth fit differ substantially.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010)**  
Pay is positively but only modestly related to job satisfaction. A somewhat higher salary should probably not dominate the decision if both options already clear your financial needs.

**Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023)**  
Emotional well-being generally rises with income, though the pattern varies and may flatten for less happy people. This makes money relevant but unlikely to outweigh large differences in daily work quality, autonomy, or meaning.

**Focalism: A Source of Durability Bias in Affective Forecasting — Wilson, Wheatley, Meyers, Gilbert & Axsom (2000)**  
People overestimate the long-term emotional impact of whichever future feature is most salient. In your case, both “more interesting work” and “finally leading a team” could be focal features that obscure mundane realities.

**On the Meaning of Work: A Theoretical Integration and Review — Rosso, Dekas & Wrzesniewski (2010)**  
Meaningful work can come from self-expression, mastery, values, belonging, and contribution to others. Leading and developing people can be meaningful even when the underlying project is less technically exciting.

**The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work — Bunderson & Thompson (2009)**  
People with deeply meaningful work often accept sacrifices in pay, conditions, and boundaries. This cautions that “follow what is interesting or meaningful” can be powerful but also exploitable if treated as the only criterion.

**The Value of Bosses — Lazear, Shaw & Stanton (2015)**  
Boss quality has large effects on worker productivity, showing that management can be a high-leverage activity. If Anthropic’s role gives real leadership scope and good organizational support, the managerial component may be a serious positive rather than a side benefit.

**Interest Fit and Job Satisfaction: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis** — Hoff et al. (2020)  
People are substantially more satisfied when their work matches their interests. This supports putting serious weight on “interesting work,” but it does not imply interest should dominate all other factors.

**Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis** — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014)  
Intrinsic motivation predicts performance strongly, especially quality and complex work outcomes. Extrinsic incentives also matter, so the evidence favors balancing interest with pay, role, and incentives rather than focusing on interest almost exclusively.

**Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory** — Hackman & Oldham (1976)  
Jobs are more motivating when they provide skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. “Interestingness” overlaps with these features, but leadership scope and autonomy may also be major sources of motivation.

**The Validity of the Job Characteristics Model: A Review and Meta-Analysis** — Fried & Ferris (1987)  
Core job characteristics reliably predict job satisfaction and internal work motivation. The paper supports choosing richer, more engaging work, but not reducing the decision to topical interest alone.

**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature** — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007)  
Motivational work features like autonomy, task significance, and feedback are strongly related to satisfaction, commitment, and lower turnover. Social features and work context also matter, suggesting team leadership and organizational environment are relevant.

**A Review of Self-Determination Theory’s Basic Psychological Needs at Work** — Van den Broeck et al. (2016)  
Work outcomes improve when jobs satisfy autonomy, competence, and relatedness needs. Interesting research may satisfy competence and autonomy, while leading a team may satisfy autonomy, impact, and relatedness.

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation** — Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999)  
Controlling extrinsic rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks. This cautions against optimizing only for salary, but it does not show salary is irrelevant.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature** — Judge et al. (2010)  
Pay has a positive but relatively modest relationship with job and pay satisfaction. This suggests a somewhat higher salary should matter, but probably less than work content, autonomy, and fit once compensation is already high.

**Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion** — Kahneman et al. (2006)  
People tend to overestimate how much higher income will improve experienced happiness. The paper supports discounting salary differences somewhat, especially when choosing between already well-paid roles.

**High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being** — Kahneman & Deaton (2010)  
Higher income improves life evaluation, while emotional well-being shows diminishing returns beyond moderate income levels. For a high-earning researcher, daily work experience may matter more than incremental compensation for day-to-day happiness.

**Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year** — Killingsworth (2021)  
Experienced well-being continues to rise with income, though roughly logarithmically. This complicates the “money barely matters” view and argues against ignoring compensation entirely.

**Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research** — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012)  
People perform better and persist more when their vocational interests fit their work. This supports prioritizing interesting, well-matched work in research-heavy careers.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit** — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005)  
Fit with the job, organization, team, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and turnover intentions. The broader lesson is not “choose the most interesting work,” but “choose the best overall fit.”

**Assessing the Work Environment for Creativity** — Amabile et al. (1996)  
Creativity is supported by challenging work, autonomy, resources, encouragement, and low organizational impediments. For research engineering, the environment around the work may be as important as whether the nominal topic is interesting.

**The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work** — Bunderson & Thompson (2009)  
Deeply meaningful work can produce commitment, identity, and satisfaction. It can also lead people to accept sacrifices, underweight compensation, and tolerate worse conditions, so “follow interest almost exclusively” has real risks.
